coredata.dc:title: An all-graphene quantum dot Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) probe for ratiometric detection of HE4 ovarian cancer biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Bharathi, Ganapathi
Lin, Fangrui
Liu, Liwei
Ohulchanskyy, Tymish Y.
Hu, Rui
Qu, Junle
coredata.dc:description: Ovarian cancer (OVC), the most lethal form of all gynecological cancers, is a big threat to women’s health. Late diagnosis at the advanced stages is one of the major reasons for the ovarian cancer-related deaths. Conventionally, the up-regulated proteins CA125 (cancer antigen 125) and HE4 (human epididymis protein 4) are used as biomarkers to diagnose the OVC malignancies. The lack of sensitivity/specificity and the false-positive results create complexity in the diagnostic process. With specificity over 90 %, HE4 is suitable for diagnosing ovarian cancer. Herein, we have developed an ultrasensitive all-graphene quantum dot (GQD) Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) probe for the ratiometric detection of HE4 biomarker. A set of two GQD samples were solvothermally prepared and then analyzed by the morphological, structural, and photophysical characterization. One GQD sample exhibited a strong green emission, peaked at around 515 nm, while the other GQD sample displayed a strong red emission with maximum at around 615 nm. The good spectral overlap between the emission and excitation spectra of the green and red GQDs, respectively, all allowed us to consider them for the design of FRET-based probe. The green and red-emitting GQDs were conjugated with HE4 antibody and used as donor and acceptor, respectively for the ratiometric sensing of HE4 ovarian cancer biomarker. The all GQD FRET probe was able to detect as low as 4.8 pM, along with a large dynamic detection range up to 300 nM. The selectivity and interference effect of the developed FRET probe was also investigated against different protein combinations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot (GQD)
Ovarian cancer
Förster
Resonance energy transfer (FRET)
HE4 biomarker
Ratiometric
FRET detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (a) gGQDs and (b) rGQDs. The average size of the GQDs is around 5 nm and the measured lattice spacing values are around 0.24 nm for both gGQDs and rGQDs. Scale bar represents 2 nm (in both a & b). (c) Raman spectra and (d) XPS survey spectra of gGQDs and rGQDs. The Raman spectra show the presence of DD- and G-bands in both the samples. The XPS survey spectra show peaks at C1s, N1s and O1s positions, indicating the formation of graphene quantum dot structures.

XPS analysis of gGQDs and rGQDs. (a), (b) and (c) represent deconvoluted high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra of the gGQD sample, respectively. (d), (e) and (f) represent deconvoluted high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra of rGQD sample, respectively. The increase of nitrogen content in the rGQD sample can be identified by the rise of C-N in the corresponding C1s spectra. The N1s spectra of the rGQDs also show the increase of pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen species in the sample.

Optical properties of GQDs. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, (b) & (c) PL emission contour profile of gGQDs and rGQDs, respectively, indicating the excitation independent emission in both the GQD samples. Inset of (a) represent the fluorescence photographic images of gGQDs and rGQDs under UV light illumination. The PL emission maximum of gGQD is at around 515 nm, and for rGQD, the emission maximum is at around 615 nm. (d) & (e) PL decays for gGQDs and rGQDs, respectively. (f) Schematic representation of the optical transitions which are responsible for green and red emissions. O* and N* represent the oxygen-related and nitrogen-related defect states, respectively.
Schematic representation of the ratiometric FRET sensing mechanism. When the HE4 antigen was added, the energy transfer occurs between the HE4 antibody conjugated gGQDs and rGQDs.

Sensing properties of the GQD FRET probe against HE4 OVC biomarker. (a) ratiometric PL sensing graph, (b) Stern-Volmer plot showing the sensing response in the range of 4 pM to 300 nM. (c) Stern-Volmer plot in the range of 0.04 – 0.4 nM, used for calculating the limit of detection. (d) Barchart showing the sensing selectivity towards HE4, amongst individuals and a combination of different proteins. Error bars were calculated based on three independent experiments.
Comparison of reported OVC cancer biomarker sensing experiments.

row.entry: Carbon dots functionalized with aptamer - PAMAM-Dendrimers functionalized Au NPs
Quantum dot nanospheres and Fluorescent nanoparticles

9.4 U/mL for CA1, 250.16 ng/mL
(5.33 pM*) for HE4

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: manually calculated using the molecular weight of 30 kDa.

head.ce:title: An all-graphene quantum dot Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) probe for ratiometric detection of HE4 ovarian cancer biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ovarian cancer (OVC), the most lethal form of all gynecological cancers, is a big threat to women’s health. Late diagnosis at the advanced stages is one of the major reasons for the ovarian cancer-related deaths. Conventionally, the up-regulated proteins CA125 (cancer antigen 125) and HE4 (human epididymis protein 4) are used as biomarkers to diagnose the OVC malignancies. The lack of sensitivity/specificity and the false-positive results create complexity in the diagnostic process. With specificity over 90 %, HE4 is suitable for diagnosing ovarian cancer. Herein, we have developed an ultrasensitive all-graphene quantum dot (GQD) Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) probe for the ratiometric detection of HE4 biomarker. A set of two GQD samples were solvothermally prepared and then analyzed by the morphological, structural, and photophysical characterization. One GQD sample exhibited a strong green emission, peaked at around 515 nm, while the other GQD sample displayed a strong red emission with maximum at around 615 nm. The good spectral overlap between the emission and excitation spectra of the green and red GQDs, respectively, all allowed us to consider them for the design of FRET-based probe. The green and red-emitting GQDs were conjugated with HE4 antibody and used as donor and acceptor, respectively for the ratiometric sensing of HE4 ovarian cancer biomarker. The all GQD FRET probe was able to detect as low as 4.8 pM, along with a large dynamic detection range up to 300 nM. The selectivity and interference effect of the developed FRET probe was also investigated against different protein combinations.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is the one of the deadliest diseases that caused about 9.6 million deaths, globally in the year of 2018 [1]. Rated as overall-fifth and first amongst the gynecological cancers, ovarian cancer has the worst prognosis with the highest mortality rate [2–4]. It is estimated that, by the year of 2035, the ovarian cancer cases will increase up to 371,000 (a 55 % increase compared to the year 2012), and eventually the deaths will increase to 254,000 (a 67 % increase compared to the year 2012) [5]. Reportedly, around 70 % of the cancer-related deaths are in middle and low-income countries [1], revealing that nearly one-third of the cancer deaths are due to the inaccessibility of diagnostic and/or treatment facilities. The development of cost-effective and simple diagnostic protocols could partially address this issue. By diagnosing at the early stages (I & II), the 5-year survival rate of OVC (which is currently less than 50 %) can be increased up to 90 % [6–9]. However, in most cases, the OVC progresses into the advanced stages, before diagnosed.

Cancer is an uncontrollably growing variation of normal cells and it does not produce anything characteristic, except the up/down regulations of few native proteins and/or other biological components. These variable biological components have been identified as biomarkers and used for the diagnosis of respective malignancies [10]. This leads to a complexity of the diagnosis processes, resulting in false-positive results, since the target analyte (biomarkers) could also be found in healthy individuals. Therefore, ultrasensitive biosensors with high selectivity must be developed. There are several methods reported for detection of the two FDA-approved (Food and Drug Administration, USA) OVC serum biomarkers CA125 and HE4 [3,11]. Somayeh Hamd-Ghadareh et al. [12], developed an aptamer-based FRET probe using carbon dots and Au nanoparticles and demonstrated the sensitive detection of CA125 with the detection limit of 5.0 fg/mL. Chanhee Han et al. [13], developed a combined biomarker panel for the simultaneous detection of four OVC biomarkers using the commercial multi-analyte immunoassay platform. Ryan M. Williams et al. [3] reported the in-vivo HE4 sensor implant constructed using carbon nanotubes.

Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is a quantum mechanical process, in which the energy from an excited donor is non-radiatively transferred to a ground state acceptor within a close distance of fewer than 10 nm [14]. It is because of this distance, FRET is widely used in biology to study the molecular interactions that usually take place within proximity. In biological sensing, FRET-based probes play an inevitable role as they can monitor the conformational changes of various signaling molecules, phosphorylation and so on [12,15–17]. When it comes to biomolecular detection, the FRET probes have been demonstrating excellent sensitivity and selectivity. For example, Anisa Kaur et al. [18], constructed a single-molecule FRET probe for the multiplexed detection of nucleic acids. The FRET efficiency mainly depends on the optical properties of the donor and acceptor, i.e., how well the acceptor absorbs the energy from the donor, depending on the spectral match and donor-acceptor distance. The broad absorption and strong fluorescence emission are the key factors for achieving a better FRET system.

Quantum dots are considered to be a good choice for constructing the FRET probes, with advantages of broad absorption and stokes-shifted strong PL emission [19,20]. Besides, the size-tunable optical behavior of quantum dot systems is an added advantage to use the QDs as a FRET component (either donor or acceptor). In other words, one can tune the absorption (excitation)/emission of a QD system by varying its size, to have a better spectral overlap between the donor emission and acceptor absorption/excitation. However, care must be taken while using the inorganic QD systems in biological detection, because of the compatibility and cytotoxicity issues [19–21]. Alternatively, carbon-based nanoparticulate systems, such as carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are well-known for their excellent optical properties, such as broad absorption and strong/stable fluorescence, and biocompatibility [16,17,22]. However, they are rarely used in design of the FRET probes. This pitfall arises due to the difficulty in tuning the optical properties. The green and red PL emission in CDs and GQDs are majorly structure-dependent, which originate from the oxygen- and nitrogen related structural defects [23–28]. Therefore, it is hard to tune the emission spectral position, to achieve the spectral overlap.

In the present work, we prepared green and red-emitting GQDs, in such a way that the emission profile of green-emitting GQDs is overlapping with the excitation of red-emitting GQDs. Using these favorable optical properties, we have developed an all graphene quantum dot FRET probe for detection of the HE4 biomarker. The green and red-emitting GQDs were used as donor and acceptor, respectively. The morphological and structural characterizations were performed to identify the formation and chemical structure of the GQDs. After conjugation with HE4 antibody, the donor and acceptor were combined in a mixture and the HE4 sensing experiments were performed. The selectivity of the developed GQD FRET probe was tested against different protein molecules. The obtained results were discussed in detail.

The p-Phenylenediamine was purchased from TCI chemicals. The solvents (like ethanol, methanol, and dichloromethane) were purchased from Aladdin chemicals. The 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were purchased from Macklin chemicals. The Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was purchased from Gibco. The HE4 antibody and HE4 recombinant protein were purchased from RayBiotech.

The green and red-emitting GQDs were prepared by the solvothermal method. Briefly, 0.1 g of p-Phenylenediamine was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol and treated in an autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down naturally, the sample was collected and purified by column chromatography using methanol and dichloromethane as eluents. The column chromatography separation process yielded both green and red-emitting graphene quantum dots. The purified samples were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The HRTEM measurements were carried out using a field emission transmission electron microscope JEOL-F200. The XPS measurements were performed in a VG ESCALAB 220i-XL system. The RAMAN spectroscopic measurements were performed using a WiTec Raman microscope system equipped with a 532 nm laser. The Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were carried out using a ThermoScientific Nicolet instrument. The UV–vis absorption measurements were performed with a Cintra 2020 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence measurements were carried out using a Horiba JobinYvon Fluorocube instrument. The PL decay curves were recorded using the Horiba JobinYvon Fluorocube instrument with the NanoLED 405 nm and 455 nm excitation sources.

The HE4 antibodies were conjugated with graphene quantum dots using the following procedure. All the solutions, dilutions, and sensing experiments were performed in 0.1 M PBS solution with pH 7.4. The GQD stock solutions were prepared with a concentration of 0.75 mg/mL. A 30 μl of EDC (100 mmol) was added to the 100 μl of GQD solution and subjected to gentle stirring at room temperature for 10 min. Later, a 30 μl of NHS (100 mmol) was added and the stirring was continued to another 30 min. Finally, a 100 μl of HE4 antibody solution (50 μg/mL) was added and stirred for another 120 mitues at room temperature. After that, the conjugated solution was kept at 4 °C overnight, to deactivate the unreacted EDC and NHS. The HE4 antibody conjugated GQDs were purified by the dialysis process, before conducting the sensing experiments.

The HE4 sensing experiments were performed using the purified HE4 Ab-GQD donor and acceptor solutions. The gGQD-Ab and rGQD-Ab solutions were mixed and subjected to PL emission measurements under 425 nm excitation wavelength. The HE4 recombinant protein was added (in the range of 4.3 pM to 300 nM) to the GQD-Ab solution and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min for each trial before subjecting to the PL emission measurements. The intensity ratios of the green and red emission peaks (I615/I515) was used to calculate the sensing efficiency. The detection limit was calculated using the equation [29], Detection limit = 3σ/K, Where σ is the standard deviation of I515/I615 and K is the quenching constant (slope).

The FRET probe for the detection of HE4 OVC biomarker was designed and constructed using green-emitting graphene quantum dots (gGQDs) as donor and red-emitting graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) as acceptor. The basic condition for the FRET process is the spectral match between the donor emission and acceptor excitation/absorption, which is satisfied well by the current choice of materials.

The as-prepared GQD samples were first subjected to morphological analysis by HRTEM. The electron microscopic images of the as-prepared samples are presented in Fig. 1 . The HRTEM images reveal that the samples are spherical-shaped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with an average size of 5 nm. The measured lattice spacing value of 0.24 nm is consistent with the reported values, corresponding to GQDs [30,31]. The formation of GQDs was further probed by the Raman and XPS analyses. The Raman spectra presented in Fig. 1c show the presence of characteristic D and G bands at 1353 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, respectively. The D and G bands are originating from the E2g mode and A1g mode phonons. It has to be noted that the G band of rGQDs shows a redshift to 1562 cm−1, which is due to the softening of phonons caused by the addition of more free electrons in the graphene structure [32–34]. This effect is attributed to the incorporation of more nitrogen (as observed from the XPS analysis), which might add more free electrons to the graphene network.

The GQD samples were then subjected to the XPS analysis, in order to analyze the chemical composition and structure. The XPS survey spectra presented in Fig. 1d and the peak table presented in supplementary Table ST1, indicate the formation of highly carbonaceous samples with dominating carbon content over 80 % in the gGQD sample and over 70 % in the rGQD sample, respectively. Further, to probe the binding nature of the identified elements, the high-resolution spectra were recorded at C1s, N1s, and O1s positions for the as-prepared GQD samples. The measured C1s spectra for gGQD and rGQD samples were deconvoluted and displayed in Fig. 2 . The deconvolution shows the presence of graphitic C=C/C–C at 284.6 eV, C–N at 285.1 eV and C=O at 288.2 eV, which are consistent with the reported values for the graphene systems [35–37].

While the C1s spectrum of the gGQD sample is dominated by the graphitic C=C/C–C, a considerable increase of C–N contribution is observed for the rGQD sample, which indicates the incorporation of more nitrogen into the graphene network. The increase of nitrogen incorporation is also observed from the deconvoluted N1s spectrum displayed in Fig. 2b & e, which indicates the presence of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen species at 398.6 eV, 399.3 eV and 400.1 eV (400.4 eV for rGQDs), respectively, in the GQD samples [36,37]. In particular, the pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen species are found to be increasing in the rGQD sample. These new nitrogen bonds are responsible for the structural changes in the graphene network, which in turn produced the red emission in the corresponding sample. The O1s spectrum of the gGQD sample was deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 532.2 eV and 533.5 eV corresponding to C=O and C–O bonds, respectively. The additional deconvoluted peak found in the rGQD sample at 534.2 eV indicates the presence of COH bonds in the corresponding sample [38–40]. The XPS observations collectively indicate that the GQD samples are composed of a carbon-rich graphitic network with minimal oxygen and nitrogen species incorporated within.

The GQD samples were then subjected to UV–vis absorption spectroscopy analysis and the measured UV–vis absorption spectra are presented in Fig. 3 a. Both the samples displayed common absorption bands at 250 nm and 280 nm, corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions of C=C and C=O/C=N species, respectively, which are the characteristic absorption bands of graphene quantum dots, as reported elsewhere [41]. In addition, a broad absorption centered at 405 nm was observed for the green fluorescent sample and is attributed to the oxygen-related defects [27,28]. Similarly, the broad absorption band observed at 485 nm for the red fluorescent sample, is attributed to the nitrogen-related defects (due to the formation of more C–N bonds) [23,25,26]. Further, the green and red fluorescent samples were subjected to Photoluminescence (PL) emission and excitation analyses. The PL contour plot of the gGQD sample presented in Fig. 3b, shows a strong green emission with maximum at around 515 nm. Similarly, the red fluorescent sample showed a strong red emission peaked at around 615 nm (Fig. 3c). We believe that the electronic transitions which produced the 405 nm and 485 nm absorption bands in green and red fluorescent samples, respectively, are responsible for the corresponding strong green and red emissions. This is further supported by the PL excitation spectra measured at respective emission positions (presented in Supplementary Fig. S1), which are overlapping with the corresponding absorption bands.

Graphene quantum dots are known to be mostly blue emitting and the PL emission mechanism is reportedly a complex one. It is because, the blue emission has contributions from edge states, confined sp2 domains, zig-zag sites, carboxyl functional groups and size effects [35,37,42,43]. In contrast, the green and red emitting GQDs are reported by only a few groups, as they are relatively difficult to obtain. However, these green and red emitting GQDs are more suitable for biological applications. The green and red emission can be induced in graphene quantum dots by doping and/or generating defect structures within the graphene network, as reported elsewhere [23,25–28,44–46]. Similarly, in our GQD samples, the origins of green and red emissions were attributed to the oxygen-related (C–O/C=O) and nitrogen-related (C–N/C=N) defect structures, respectively. In addition, both the GQDs exhibit excitation independent emission behavior, which indicates the presence of common recombination centers producing green and red fluorescence in corresponding samples. This observation is consistent with the reported graphene quantum dot and carbon dot systems that exhibited green and red emissions [23,26,44,46]. The measured PL decays are found to be monoexponential (Fig. 3d & e), which further supports the common origin of the emission in both the green and red emitting GQD samples. The average PL lifetimes are 8.2 ns and 7.5 ns for gGQDs and rGQDs, respectively. The schematic representation of the possible optical transitions, which are responsible for the green and red emissions, is shown in Fig. 3f.

The FRET probe was constructed using the gGQDs as a donor and the rGQDs as an acceptor (the detailed procedure is given in the experimental section). The spectral overlap graph given in supplementary Fig. S1 shows the overlapping of gGQD emission with rGQD excitation, which is essential for constructing a FRET probe. The schematic representation of the FRET process between gGQD donor and rGQD acceptor is presented in Fig. 4 . In the HE4 antibody conjugated GQDs mixture solution, upon addition, the HE4 antigen binds with two antibodies, thereby bringing the gGQDs and rGQDs in proximity to produce the FRET-induced red emission.

The HE4 antibodies were conjugated to gGQDs and rGQDs via the widely used EDC/NHS mediated conjugation reaction [3,16,17]. After conjugation, the donor and acceptor were purified by the dialysis process, separately, and subjected to the FTIR analysis, in order to identify the conjugation. In general, the conjugation of anitibody via carboxylate group of GQD and amine group of antibody takes place through the formation of an amide bond as represented in Fig S2. During the conjugation process, a new C–N bond formation (O=C–N–H) would take place and consequently there arises a new peak. In our case, we have observed the corresponding new peak at 1248 cm−1. In addition, the CO bond peak is observed at 1670-1690 cm−1, with a shift upon bond formation (supplementary Fig. S3). The observed peak shift is small (around 10 cm−1), which could be due to the contribution of some additional C=O groups present in the GQD molecular structure. The FTIR results thereby confirm the successful GQD-Ab conjugation [47,48].

The UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of pure and HE4 antibody conjugated GQDs are presented in supplementary Fig. S4. The conjugated gGQDs-Ab and rGQD-Ab samples were mixed by gentle shaking and then subjected to sensing experiments. In order to detect the HE4 OVC biomarker, the HE4 antigen was diluted to different concentrations viz., 4 pM to 300 nM, and added to the GQD-Ab FRET probe. The GQD-Ab FRET probe was incubated at 37 oC after each addition of HE4 antigen and the PL emission spectrum was recorded correspondingly. The PL emission spectra presented in Fig. 5 a show the occurrence of ratiometrically detectable FRET between the gGQDs and rGQDs, i.e., the green emission from the gGQD donor was quenching with a corresponding increase in the red emission from the rGQD acceptor. The linear sensing behavior of our GQD-Ab FRET probe was analyzed by plotting the ratio between the intensities of green and red emission bands (I615/I515) against the concentration of HE4 antigen and the resultant graph was displayed in Fig. 5b. This indicates the large dynamic sensing response range of our FRET probe from 4 pM to 300 nM. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 4.8 pM, which is much less than the HE4 levels found in the OVC patients [3,49,50]. Therefore the newly developed GQD-FRET probe can be applied to the clinical measurement trails for measuring the HE4 levels in the healthy individuals and OVC patients. Our results were compared with the previously reported data on probes for the detection of ovarian cancer biomarkers (CA125 and HE4); the comparison results are presented in Table 1 .

In general, the biosensor must have high selectivity in order to avoid false-positive results. The selectivity of our GQD-FRET probe was tested by the addition of other proteins, viz., CA125 and BSA. The results presented in Fig. 5d indicate that our GQD-FRET probe is highly selective towards HE4, with a selectivity over 95 %. In addition, the interference effect of other proteins on the selectivity/sensitivity was investigated by performing the sensing experiments against different proteins at different combinations, viz., CA125 + BSA, CA125 + HE4, BSA + HE4, CA125 + BSA + HE4. The bar chart shown in Fig. 5d, indicates that our GQD-FRET probe is an interference-free detection system, i.e, the sensitivity was not affected by the co-added proteins.

In summary, the all GQD-FRET probe was constructed successfully and used for the detection of HE4 OVC biomarker. By choosing the green and red-emitting GQDs, the fundamental criteria for the FRET phenomenon were achieved. The HE4 antibody conjugated green and red-emitting GQDs were used as FRET-based fluorescent probe. The developed GQD-FRET probe demonstrated high selectivity towards HE4 antigen with a detection limit of 4.8 pM. The GQD-FRET probe could be used to determine the HE4 levels quantitatively, in the OVC patient serum samples. Due to a simple design, similar all-GQD FRET probe can be developed to detect biomarkers related to other severe cancers.

Ganapathi Bharathi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Fangrui Lin: Methodology. Liwei Liu: Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Tymish Y. Ohulchanskyy: Writing - review & editing. Rui Hu: Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Junle Qu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest related to this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China(61961136005/61722508/61935012/61525503/61620106016/61835009), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019M653000), ( Key) Project of Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD007), and Shenzhen Science and Technology Funding(JCYJ20180305124902165/JCYJ20170412105003520). The author GB thank Dr. P. Naveen, Department of Chemistry, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, TN, India for his valuable suggestions and discussions in the FTIR interpretation.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2020.111458.
